audit_trail.notes: All six reviews (three reviewers across two cycles) were conducted on the abstract only; the full Phys. Rev. D article is behind a subscription paywall. Reviewer confidence is uniformly low (3–5.2/10 across cycle 2) due to this limitation. All three reviewers converged independently on the same concern hierarchy: paywall inaccessibility, pressure omission, magnetic field omission. One notable inter-reviewer calibration issue was corrected between cycles: gemini's cycle-1 reviewer_confidence of 8/10 was flagged as inconsistent with abstract-only access and revised to 4/10 in cycle 2. The paper's publication in Phys. Rev. D after peer review (received October 2024, accepted April 2025) was noted by claude as weak positive evidence that the pressure omission may be defended in the body, but this could not be verified. No reviewer argued the paper is certainly wrong; all treated the main objections as unresolved pending full-text access.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper asks whether a tiny black hole formed inside a star from captured dark matter could swallow the star whole, and whether some stars might resist this fate through rotation and friction. The review board found the research question legitimate and the paper published in a reputable peer-reviewed journal (Physical Review D), but reviewers could only access the abstract—the full text is paywalled. The most important unresolved concern is that the model explicitly skips pressure, which is the dominant force inside the very dense stars being studied; whether the authors defend this omission in the paper body cannot be determined from what was available.
human_spotchecks: Does the paper body provide an explicit order-of-magnitude argument or controlled limiting regime demonstrating that pressure gradients are subleading relative to centrifugal or viscous forces in the specific parameter regime used for the WD and NS stalling analysis? This is the single most important technical question and would determine whether the pressure omission is a defended approximation or an unphysical assumption.

Is the rotation-viscosity framework derived specifically for internal (endoparasitic) accretion geometry—with appropriate boundary conditions for a black hole surrounded by host stellar material on all sides—or is it adapted from standard external alpha-disk or slim-disk models, which assume an inner vacuum boundary?

What dark matter model (particle mass, spin, interaction cross-section, thermalization prescription) is assumed, and what are the implied EBH formation timescales relative to white dwarf and neutron star ages? Are results presented as conditional on an already-formed EBH, or as astrophysically predictive?
Do the two papers that cite this work (identified in APS metrics) validate, critique, extend, or contradict the stalling result and the WD-vs-NS distinction?

Is the polar opening derived from a dynamical hydrodynamic model, or inferred qualitatively from the accretion geometry? Does the paper quantify the opening angle as a function of host mass and spin with explicit parameter dependence?
public_summary:
Adarsha, Chakraborty, and Bhattacharyya (Phys. Rev. D 111, 103033, May 2025) study accretion by an endoparasitic black hole (EBH)—a microscopic black hole hypothesized to form when dark matter particles accumulate and collapse at the core of a white dwarf or neutron star. Motivated by gravitational wave events (GW190425, GW190814) whose component masses may include near-subsolar black holes, the paper argues that rotation and viscosity can stall accretion in some white dwarfs but not neutron stars, with stalled accretion leaving a characteristic polar opening in the host. The EBH concept has a multi-decade prior literature; the claimed novelty is adding rotation and viscosity to the internal-accretion framework and deriving the differential WD vs. NS outcome.

The review board's three independent reviewers worked from the abstract only, as the full article is behind a subscription paywall. All three converged on the same dominant concern: the analysis explicitly omits pressure, which is the primary restoring force inside white dwarfs (electron degeneracy pressure) and neutron stars (nuclear/neutron degeneracy pressure), and also governs the sound speed that sets Bondi-type accretion rates. Whether this omission is defended by an order-of-magnitude scale argument or controlled limiting regime inside the paper body—or is simply assumed away—cannot be determined from the abstract. The paper's publication in PRD after peer review provides weak evidence that referees may have required justification, but does not resolve the question for this board. Magnetic field omission is a secondary concern, particularly for the neutron-star conclusion, as magnetospheric effects can independently prevent accretion regardless of viscosity. The dark matter model and implied EBH formation timescales are also unstated in the abstract, making the astrophysical scope unclear.
reviewer_disagreements: Gemini assigned reviewer_confidence 8/10 in cycle 1 despite having access only to the abstract; claude flagged this as overconfident, arguing that high confidence about the severity of the pressure omission requires knowing whether the paper body defends or quantifies the approximation. Gemini corrected to 4/10 in cycle 2.

Gemini rated the magnetic field omission as 'major' severity in cycle 2, escalating from the 'moderate' rating assigned by both claude and codex across both cycles. Codex also cautioned that the propeller-effect analogy is specific to external magnetospheric disk accretion and may not apply directly to an internal EBH accreting from the stellar interior.

Gemini in cycle 2 rated the paywall inaccessibility as 'fatal' severity; claude and codex consistently rated it 'major' but treated it as a process limitation rather than a technical flaw, noting that PRD peer review makes an entirely undefended approximation less likely.

Codex treated the stalling claims as 'unclear' (acknowledging possible body-level justification) rather than 'unsupported' as gemini labeled them, arguing that declaring the model invalid requires reading the inaccessible derivation and that the body may include a controlled limiting approximation.

strongest_objections.objection: The full article text is inaccessible due to a subscription paywall; only the abstract is available for review. All quantitative claims—the stalling criterion, the polar opening geometry, the rotation-viscosity framework, and the parameter domain—reside in the inaccessible body. This is a process limitation that prevents raising reviewer confidence above low levels and renders all technical assessments provisional.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Full text (inaccessible); bundle source text shows 'Authorization Required' immediately after abstract

strongest_objections.objection: The model explicitly omits pressure in an analysis of accretion inside pressure-dominated compact objects. White dwarfs are supported by electron degeneracy pressure and neutron stars by nuclear/neutron degeneracy pressure; these pressures also set the sound speed that governs Bondi-type accretion rates (rate scales as c_s^{-3}). Omitting pressure may qualitatively alter whether stalling occurs at all, not merely shift the threshold quantitatively. The 'first step' framing is scientifically honest but does not by itself constitute a justification for the approximation.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract: 'ignoring some other effects, such as those of pressure and magnetic field, as the first step'

strongest_objections.objection: Magnetic fields are omitted from an analysis that concludes accretion is 'not stalled' in neutron stars. Neutron star surface fields range from 10^8 to 10^15 G; magnetospheric accretion physics and the propeller effect can prevent accretion independently of viscosity. The non-stalling conclusion for neutron stars may be contingent on this omission.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract: 'ignoring some other effects, such as those of pressure and magnetic field, as the first step'; and final result sentence on neutron stars

strongest_objections.objection: The white-dwarf versus neutron-star stalling contrast is stated without visible parameter hypotheses: the stellar equation of state, rotation law (uniform vs. differential), viscosity prescription, seed EBH mass, and GR treatment are all absent from the abstract. The scope of the result—which white dwarfs, under which conditions—is undefined in the reviewable text.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract, final result sentence: 'some white dwarfs, but not in neutron stars'

strongest_objections.objection: The dark matter model assumed for the capture-thermalization-collapse chain is not stated in the abstract. EBH formation timescales and collapse criteria are highly parameter-sensitive (DM mass, interaction cross-section, thermalization rate), and results valid for one DM candidate may not generalize. This affects the astrophysical scope and predictive value of the paper.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Abstract, sentences 3–4 describing EBH formation mechanism

strongest_supported_claims: Subsolar-mass black holes cannot form through standard stellar evolution; this is a well-established result that motivates exotic formation channels such as EBH formation from dark matter capture.

The endoparasitic black hole mechanism—dark matter capture, thermalization, and gravitational collapse inside a stellar host—is a legitimate speculative proposal with a multi-decade prior literature (Goldman and Nussinov 1989 and subsequent work).
The paper's framing of GW190425 and GW190814 as possible near-solar mass black hole events is one of several competing interpretations of those events and is fair as motivational context.

Incorporating rotation and viscosity into the EBH internal-accretion framework is an incremental but recognized gap in the prior literature, assuming the framework and pressure-omission justification are sound.
technical_takeaway: The paper claims to derive a rotation-viscosity framework for endoparasitic black hole (EBH) accretion inside compact stellar hosts and argues accretion stalls in some white dwarfs but not neutron stars, with stalled accretion producing polar openings. All three reviewers across two cycles independently converged on the same dominant concern: the explicit omission of pressure from a model of accretion inside pressure-dominated objects (white dwarfs supported by electron degeneracy pressure, neutron stars by nuclear pressure) is a qualitatively significant simplification that could alter not just the magnitude but the existence of stalling, since pressure sets the sound speed governing Bondi-type accretion. Magnetic field omission is a secondary concern, particularly for the neutron-star non-stalling conclusion. PRD peer-review publication is weak positive evidence that these issues may be addressed in the body, but remains unverifiable from the abstract alone. Reviewer confidence is uniformly low due to paywall-only access.
